original_abstract:
Nanoparticle (NP) synthesis has rapidly shifted toward green nanotechnology, driven by the need for sustainable alternatives to costly and often toxic physical-chemical methods. Among metal oxides, zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO-NPs) are particularly prized for their unique thermal, optical, and electronic properties. This study presents an eco-friendly synthesis of ZnO-NPs using a polyphenol-rich extract from *Phoenix dactylifera* L. (date palm) as a natural reducing agent. 

To optimize the synthesis, we examined how varying the concentrations of both the precursor and the reducing agent influenced the resulting nanomaterials. The synthesized NPs were systematically characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Our results confirm the successful formation of crystalline ZnO-NPs with particle sizes ranging from 18.1 to 61.6 nm. Beyond structural characterization, the nanoparticles demonstrated significant antioxidant potential—as assessed by TAC and DPPH assays—and effective antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive (*Staphylococcus aureus*) and Gram-negative (*Escherichia coli*) pathogenic strains. These findings suggest that plant-mediated synthesis is a viable, high-performance substitute for traditional toxic methodologies in the production of functional metallic nanoparticles.
humanized_abstract:
Nanoparticle (NP) synthesis is rapidly moving towards green nanotechnology due to the escalating need for economical and eco-friendly process alternatives to the conventional expensive physical-chemical methods. Moreover, the metal oxide nanoparticles have gained substantial importance due to their remarkable thermal, optical and electronic properties. In this study, eco-friendly synthesis of zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO-NPs) using aqueous date palm extract (DPE) rich in polyphenols as a green reducing agent is presented.

In order to make the synthesis process efficient, the effect of different concentrations of precursor solution and reducing agent on the prepared nanostructures was investigated. The prepared NPs were characterized by XRD, TEM, SEM and FTIR. The results show that crystalline ZnO-NPs with varying particle sizes in the range of 18.1-61.6 nm were prepared. In addition to structural studies, the obtained bio-fabricated NPs were evaluated for their antioxidant activity using total antioxidant capacity (TAC) assay and free radical scavenging activity using DPPH assay and antibacterial studies against the human pathogens Staphylococcus aureus (Gram positive) and Escherichia coli (Gram negative). The results suggest that plant-mediated synthesis can be an efficient and non-toxic method for producing active metallic nanoparticles compared with the conventional toxic chemical-based methods.

In order to make the synthesis process efficient, the effect of different concentrations of precursor solution and reducing agent on the prepared nanostructures was investigated. The prepared NPs were characterized by XRD, TEM, SEM and FTIR. The results show that crystalline ZnO-NPs with varying particle sizes in the range of 18.1-61.6 nm were prepared. In addition to structural studies, the obtained bio-fabricated NPs were evaluated for their antioxidant activity using total antioxidant capacity (TAC) assay and free radical scavenging activity using DPPH assay and antibacterial studies against the human pathogens Staphylococcus aureus (Gram positive) and Escherichia coli (Gram negative). The results suggest that plant-mediated synthesis can be an efficient and non-toxic method for producing active metallic nanoparticles compared with the conventional toxic chemical-based methods.
